coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent and ratiometric colorimetric biosensor for detection of different hazard contaminants in dairy products
coredata.dc:creator: Zuo, Wanchao
Wu, Pengcheng
He, Wei
Xiao, Qingqing
Yang, Jun
Liu, Xinmei
Jiang, Hui
Dai, Jianjun
Ju, Yanmin
coredata.dc:description: The quality and safety of dairy products are directly related to public health. However, it remains challenging to achieve detection of multi-class dairy hazard contaminants. Herein, we constructed a biosensor combining Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) with Fe3O4 @polydopamine (PDA) nanoparticles (NPs) to rapidly detect antibiotics and nitrite. Taking kanamycin (KAN) and sodium nitrite (NaNO2) as model targets, Fe-CDs were conjugated with KAN aptamers (Apt) to give aptamer-functionalized Fe-CDs (Fe-CDs-Apt). Fe-CDs-Apt could specifically recognize KAN, forming Fe-CDs-Apt-KAN complexes in the solution. Free Fe-CDs-Apt were adsorbed by subsequently added Fe3O4 @PDA NPs and separated by an external magnetic field. While, Fe-CDs-Apt-KAN kept in the supernatant and remained fluorescence intensity positively correlated with KAN concentration. NaNO2 concentration in the supernatant was monitored by ratiometric colorimetry using a nitrite-induced specific diazotization reaction. The system showed wide linear ranges of KAN (17.2 pM−343 nM) and NaNO2 (5 −5000 μM), and relatively low detection limits of 0.91 pM for KAN and 0.54 μM for NaNO2. This method could complete the detection process of the two targets within 20 min and successfully monitored KAN and NaNO2 in four kinds of dairy samples with recoveries of 92.4 − 108.7% and 90.3 − 107.2%, respectively. Biosensors based on versatile CDs and magnetic NPs appear promising in improving the analytical throughput and efficiency for food safety monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Magnetic nanoparticles
Antibiotics
Nitrite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principles of the biosensor for detection of typical antibiotics and nitrite
Fluorescence properties and peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs
Selective adsorption capacities of Fe3O4@PDA NPs on ssDNA
Parameters optimization for KAN and NaNO2 detection
Detection performances of the constructed biosensor
Analytical method validation and application in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China [Grant number 2019YFC1605400 ]; the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant numbers 52002402 , 31872479 , 32172855 ], the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province [Grant number BK20200574 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs-Apt. (A) TEM image of Fe-CDs. Scale bar: 10 nm. (B) XRD pattern of Fe-CDs. High resolution XPS spectra of (C) C 1 s; (D) N 1 s; (E) Fe 2p. (F) FT-IR spectroscopy of Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs-Apt.

Characterizations of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 @PDA NPs. TEM images of (A) Fe3O4 NPs and (B) Fe3O4 @PDA NPs. Scale bar: 100 nm. (C) Zeta-potential of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 @PDA NPs. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3O4 NPs, PDA NPs and Fe3O4 @PDA NPs (0.5 mg/mL). (E) FT-IR spectroscopy of dopamine (DA), PDA, Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 @PDA NPs. (F) Room-temperature magnetization curves of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 @PDA NPs. Inset: photo of the Fe3O4 @PDA NPs before (left) and after (right) exposed to an external magnetic field.

Fluorescence properties and peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs. (A) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Fe-CDs in aqueous solutions (0.05 mg/mL). Inset: photo of Fe-CDs in aqueous solution under visible (left) and 365 nm UV (right) light. (B) Excitation-emission matrix map of Fe-CDs. (C) Excitation-dependent fluorescence of Fe-CDs. (D) Fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs against other ionic interferences. (E) Peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs. TMB concentration was 25 mM and H2O2 concentration was 10 mM. Fe-CDs concentration was 0.5 mg/mL. (F) UV–vis spectra of the mixture of TMB-H2O2 and Fe-CDs, without or with NaNO2 (100 μM). Inset: corresponding photo of color change before (left) and after (right) adding NaNO2.

Selective adsorption capacities of Fe3O4 @PDA NPs on ssDNA. (A) Adsorption of FAM-ssDNA (100 nM) by Fe3O4 @PDA NPs (250 μg/mL). (B) The quenching efficiency of Fe3O4 @PDA NPs with different concentrations (5, 10, 50, 100, 150, 250, 300, 400, 500 μg/mL) in Fe-CDs-Apt (Fe-CDs: 0.5 mg/mL) solution. (C) Native-PAGE of DNA with different conformations and DNA treated with Fe3O4 @PDA NPs. 1: DNA Marker; 2: Apt; 3: Apt + NPs; 4: Apt-KAN; 5: Apt-KAN + NPs.

Detection performances of the constructed biosensor. (A) Fluorescence signals of the system with different KAN concentrations. (B) Linear correlation between ΔFL intensity and the logarithm of KAN concentration. Data are presented as mean ± SD. (C) Specificity evaluations for KAN detection. KAN: 85.8 nM, other antibiotics: 858 nM. (D) UV–vis spectra of the mixture of TMB-H2O2 and supernatant containing NaNO2 at different concentrations. Inset: corresponding photo of colorimetric effect with various NaNO2 concentrations; magnified view of the absorption curve at around 652 nm. (E) Calibration curve of NaNO2 detection. Inset: the corresponding linear relationship between A445 nm/A652 nm values and NaNO2 concentrations. (F) Specificity evaluations for NaNO2 detection. NaNO2: 200 μM, other contaminants: 2000 μM.

Schematic illustration of the biosensor for detecting KAN and nitrite. (a) Synthesis process of Fe-CDs-Apt and the dual roles of Fe-CDs. (b) Preparation of Fe3O4 @PDA NPs and their functions. (c) Designed biosensor applied for the detection of KAN and nitrite.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent and ratiometric colorimetric biosensor for detection of different hazard contaminants in dairy products

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Joint International Research Laboratory of Animal Health and Food Safety, Key Laboratory of Animal Bacteriology, Ministry of Agriculture, College of Veterinary Medicine, Nanjing Agricultural University, Nanjing, 210095, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Pharmacy, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmacy, China Pharmaceutical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The quality and safety of dairy products are directly related to public health. However, it remains challenging to achieve detection of multi-class dairy hazard contaminants. Herein, we constructed a biosensor combining Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) with Fe3O4 @polydopamine (PDA) nanoparticles (NPs) to rapidly detect antibiotics and nitrite. Taking kanamycin (KAN) and sodium nitrite (NaNO2) as model targets, Fe-CDs were conjugated with KAN aptamers (Apt) to give aptamer-functionalized Fe-CDs (Fe-CDs-Apt). Fe-CDs-Apt could specifically recognize KAN, forming Fe-CDs-Apt-KAN complexes in the solution. Free Fe-CDs-Apt were adsorbed by subsequently added Fe3O4 @PDA NPs and separated by an external magnetic field. While, Fe-CDs-Apt-KAN kept in the supernatant and remained fluorescence intensity positively correlated with KAN concentration. NaNO2 concentration in the supernatant was monitored by ratiometric colorimetry using a nitrite-induced specific diazotization reaction. The system showed wide linear ranges of KAN (17.2 pM−343 nM) and NaNO2 (5 −5000 μM), and relatively low detection limits of 0.91 pM for KAN and 0.54 μM for NaNO2. This method could complete the detection process of the two targets within 20 min and successfully monitored KAN and NaNO2 in four kinds of dairy samples with recoveries of 92.4 − 108.7% and 90.3 − 107.2%, respectively. Biosensors based on versatile CDs and magnetic NPs appear promising in improving the analytical throughput and efficiency for food safety monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dairy products have become an indispensable portion of the universal nutritional diet, and their quality and safety has been one of the major concerns worldwide, directly related to human health. Dairy product safety incidents such as “Milk containing antibiotics” and “Milk containing nitrite” have posed a huge impact on both consumers and the dairy industry [1,2]. Antibiotics have been widely used in veterinary medicine and feed additives for the treatment of microbial infections. However, irrationally used antibiotics can easily accumulate in human body through the food chain, producing acute or chronic toxic symptoms and allergic reactions [3–5], etc. Likewise, nitrite can be ingested by cows through feeding and water supply [6]. The International Agency for Research on Cancer has classified nitrite as a human GROUP 2 A carcinogen. The veterinary drug residues and pollutants mentioned are important hazard components in dairy products, which is undoubtedly a potential risk for human especially infants with incomplete development, poor immunity but high dairy intake [7,8]. Hence, it would be desirable to develop a sensitive method to monitor antibiotic and nitrite in dairy products.

Till now, traditional physicochemical assays (e.g. liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, ion chromatography and spectrophotometry), microbial inhibition methods, and immunoassays have been widely used for antibiotics or nitrite detection [9–12]. However, cumbersome operation, large reagent depletion, high cost and time consumption restrict their applications in food safety control [13–15]. Biosensors are flourishing detection devices with high accuracy, specificity, ease of operation, and low cost. Recently, researchers have mainly established a series of fluorescent, colorimetric and electrochemical biosensors to conveniently detect antibiotics or nitrite [16–19]. Nevertheless, these methods focus on only one major class of substances in dairy products, which barely provides limited sample information and cannot meet the need for detection of multi-class substances in daily monitoring. After all, multiple food hazards may coexist in milk products and produce greater toxicity upon entry into the human body. So, it is necessary to develop a facile, highly sensitive and efficient approach to detect multiplex hazards for dairy products monitoring.

Carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon dots (CDs), graphene and carbon nanotubes, have been extensively developed as nanozymes, and could open up new directions for the construction of multifunctional biosensing platforms due to their potential electronic, optical and thermal properties [20–22]. CDs, a zero-dimensional nanomaterial with ultra-small size less than 10 nm, hold great potential for multiplex detection due to their excellent photoluminescence properties, catalytic properties, and water dispersion [23]. However, the enzyme-like catalytic efficiency of CDs is limited by the electron transfer efficiency of the internal structure [24]. Recent studies revealed heteroatom doping could improve the catalytic properties of CDs. For example, Fe-doped carbon nanozymes led to enhanced active center utilization during peroxidase-like catalysis, which could display higher enzyme-like activity than other metal (i.e., Cu, Co, Ni, Mn)-doped carbon nanozymes [25–28]. Inspiringly, CDs integrating fluorescence and nanozyme catalytic properties can be developed for multiplex analysis in biomedicine.

DNA-functionalized nanomaterials, such as various 2D materials, metal oxide nanoparticles (NPs) and metallic NPs, have been widely explored as highly sensitive and specific biosensors [29–32]. Polydopamine (PDA) is the first reported polymer that can independently functionalize surfaces of almost all materials [33]. Notably, PDA has been shown to selectively adsorb single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) and show different affinities for ssDNA with various conformations, while failing to adsorb proteins or other biomolecules [34]. Due to this fantastic feature, DNA/PDA-based sensors can effectively resist the interference from complex sample matrix, thus allowing wider analytical applications [34,35]. Qiang et al. developed a sensing platform based on core-shell magnetic NPs@PDA nanocomposites and carboxyfluorescein (FAM) labeled ssDNA for ultra-sensitive detection of thrombin, with a detection limit of 0.5 nM, where the magnetic NPs served to simplify and accelerate the sample separation [35]. Magnetic NPs (MNPs), represented by magnetite (Fe3O4), have been widely used in biosensing owing to their superparamagnetism, good biocompatibility, and easy preparation [36,37]. As can be drawn, MNPs@PDA nanocomposites possess unique advantages in the construction of DNA-based biosensors for rapid and sensitive analysis of complex sample matrix.

In this work, we designed a biosensor combining fluorescent Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) with magnetic Fe3O4 @PDA NPs, to rapidly detect antibiotics and nitrite in dairy products for the first time ( Scheme 1). Fe-CDs not only possessed photoluminescent properties but also showed outstanding peroxidase-like activities. Fe3O4 @PDA NPs displayed high magnetic separation properties and different affinities to ssDNA with various conformations. Taking kanamycin (KAN) and sodium nitrite (NaNO2) as model targets, Fe-CDs were conjugated with KAN aptamers (Apt) to give aptamer-functionalized Fe-CDs (Fe-CDs-Apt). When KAN was present in the sample, Fe-CDs-Apt specifically recognized and bound KAN, forming Fe-CDs-Apt-KAN complexes. After the addition of Fe3O4 @PDA NPs, free Fe-CDs-Apt were selectively adsorbed by Fe3O4 @PDA NPs while Fe-CDs-Apt-KAN failed to be adsorbed and kept in the supernatant. At this time, the solution system was magnetically separated, and the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs-Apt-KAN complexes in the supernatant was measured to quantify KAN concentration. When 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) -H2O2 was added to the supernatant containing NaNO2, Fe-CDs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2, and oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) would further react with nitrite to form diazotization products. The detection results proved to be independent of each other and the detection process of the two targets could be totally completed within 20 min. Impressively, the proposed biosensor exhibited excellent linear relationships with logarithms of KAN concentrations of 17.2 pM− 343 nM, and with NaNO2 concentrations of 5 − 5000 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) values were calculated to be 0.91 pM for KAN and 0.54 μM for NaNO2, which were lower than the maximum contamination level of KAN and nitrite established by the European Union and China (150 μg kg−1 and 2.0 mg kg−1) [38]. This biosensing strategy based on versatile CDs and Fe3O4 @PDA NPs for rapid multi-target detection holds great promise in daily monitoring of dairy hazard components and food quality and safety control.
Detailed reagents and instrumentations are included in the supplementary file. All the DNA sequences are listed in Table S1.

Fe-CDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal method. 1.0507 g of citric acid (CA), 335 μL of ethylenediamine and 1.0507 g of FeCl3∙6 H2O were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water. The homogeneous solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After the mixture cooled to room temperature, the brown-black suspension was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane and further dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h.

In a typical carbodiimide method, 25 mg of 1 − (3 −Dimethylaminopropyl)− 3 −ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC HCl) and 25 mg of N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were dissolved in 5 mL of Fe-CDs (0.5 mg/mL), and the solution was kept stirring for 1 h. After the addition of 50 μL of 100 μM KAN aptamers, the mixture was incubated for another 4 h. The prepared Fe-CDs-Apt were purified via dialysis to remove excessive aptamers, and later stored in 4 °C.

Fe3O4 NPs were prepared by the high-temperature solvothermal reaction according to a previous study [39]. To coat PDA shell on Fe3O4 NPs, 20 mg of dopamine (DA) was added to 40 mL of 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.5) containing 20 mg of as-synthesized Fe3O4 NPs. After stirring for 12 h, the precipitate was magnetically separated and washed with ethanol for three times. The final products were dried in vacuum overnight.

Sterilized milk, pasteurized milk, fermented milk and infant milk powder were collected from a local supermarket. The dairy samples were treated as stated in previous studies [38,40]. Detailed procedures are included in the supplementary file.

Under optimal conditions, 50 μL of KAN containing solution and 100 μL of NaNO2 containing solution were mixed with 40 μL of Fe-CDs-Apt. The mixture was made to 300 μL with 10 mM HEPES buffer (2 mM Ca2+, pH 7.0) and incubated for 10 min. Afterwards, 100 μL of Fe3O4 @PDA NPs (1 mg/mL) was introduced into the mixture and kept incubating for 2 min. The solution system was magnetically separated. The fluorescence spectrum of the supernatant was measured (excitation at 338 nm) and the fluorescence intensity changes at 436 nm were utilized to quantify KAN. Meanwhile, 150 μL of the supernatant containing NaNO2, 50 μL of 15 mM TMB and 50 μL of 1 mM H2O2 solution were added to NaAc-HAc buffer. After a 5 −min incubation, the absorption spectra of the mixture were analyzed and nitrite was quantified by the absorbance ratio at 445 nm and 652 nm (A445 nm/A652 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principles of the biosensor for detection of typical antibiotics and nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the specificity of aptamers, Fe-CDs were modified with the amino-terminal KAN aptamer via amide bonds to achieve specific recognization for KAN. Considering that PDA NPs could selectively adsorb ssDNA and exhibit differential affinities to ssDNA with different conformations, core-shell structured Fe3O4 @PDA NPs were prepared. In absence of the target, free Fe-CDs-Apt could be adsorbed onto Fe3O4 @PDA NPs through the interaction forces such as π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding as well as Ca2+-mediated coordination [34,41]. Upon the addition of KAN, Fe-CDs-Apt would first specifically recognize and bind KAN. Fe-CDs-Apt-KAN complexes would fail to conjugate with Fe3O4 @PDA NPs due to altered configurations of aptamers. Then, the mixture was magnetically separated, and the Fe-CDs fluorescence intensity remained in the supernatant was measured to quantify KAN concentration. NaNO2 concentration in the supernatant was further monitored with TMB-H2O2 substrate solution. Under the catalysis of Fe-CDs, the solution would appear blue due to the generation of ox-TMB with a characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm. While the presence of nitrite in the supernatant would lead to a gradual color change from blue to yellow-green, which was attributed to the diazotization of nitrite with ox-TMB. The unique diazotization products had a characteristic absorption peak at 445 nm [42–45]. Hence, quantitative detection of nitrite was achieved by ratiometric colorimetry based on A445 nm/A652 nm values.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of Fe-CDs showed a uniform dispersion with 2 − 5 nm diameter ( Fig. 1A). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Fe-CDs (Fig. 1B) displayed a broad peak centered at 25°, which was ascribed to the highly disordered carbon atoms [46]. From the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum in Fig. S1, Fe-CDs were mainly composed of four elements including C 1 s (68.5%), O 1 s (19.8%), N 1 s (10.7%) and Fe 2p (1.0%) with binding energy of 284.8, 531.2, 400.0 and 710.8 eV, respectively. Particularly, C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1C) was resolved into three peaks at 284.6, 285.9 and 287.9 eV, which were ascribed to three different types of carbon atoms: graphitic or aliphatic (CC and C−C), oxygenated, and nitrous [46]. The XPS spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 1D) contained three peaks at 399.6, 400.4 and 401.4 eV, which were assigned to pyrrolic N, graphitic N and N − (C)3, respectively [47]. As shown in Fig. 1E, Fe 2p XPS spectrum could be subdivided into four types of Fe species, corresponding to Fe2+ 2p3/2 (710.5 eV), Fe3+ 2p3/2 (713.4 eV), Fe2+ 2p1/2 (723.2 eV), and Fe3+ 2p1/2 (725.9 eV), respectively, demonstrating the successful doping of Fe element [48]. Next, the successful conjugation between aptamers and Fe-CDs was verified by Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry. The bands located at 1705 cm−1 and 1605 cm−1 represented CO stretching vibration and the bending vibration of N − H in the amide, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 2720 cm−1 and 1240 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of −OH and PO stretching vibration in the phosphate molecule, respectively (Fig. 1F) [49]. Besides, the shift of zeta-potential from 6.48 mV to 2.16 mV also successfully proved their connection (Fig. S2).

The as-prepared Fe3O4 NPs showed typical XRD patterns of magnetite (PDF#19 −0629) (Fig. S3). TEM revealed that Fe3O4 NPs were well dispersed with spherical shape and had a uniform size of about 108.8 nm ( Fig. 2A). As shown in Fig. 2B, the average particle size of Fe3O4 @PDA NPs was 122.4 nm, indicating the successful coating of a uniform PDA shell layer. Similarly, zeta potential (Fig. 2C) of Fe3O4 NPs changed from − 0.677 mV to − 16.167 mV after PDA coating. From the UV–vis absorption spectra, Fe3O4 @PDA NPs exhibited significantly higher absorption values compared to Fe3O4 NPs and PDA NPs with the same concentration (Fig. 2D). In FT-IR spectroscopy, the band at 580 cm−1 of Fe−O stretching vibration, the vibrating bands at 1604 cm−1 and 1506 cm−1 of CC in the benzene rings of PDA, and the characteristic peak at 1287 cm−1 of C−O in phenolic hydroxyl groups all demonstrated the successful coating of PDA on the surface of Fe3O4 NPs (Fig. 2E) [50]. The magnetic property of the NPs was measured by vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) and found that Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 @PDA NPs possessed paramagnetic feature at room temperature. The reduction of the saturation magnetization value for Fe3O4 @PDA NPs was caused by the weight contribution of the non-magnetic PDA part. Fe3O4 @PDA NPs not only presented excellent magnetic separation effect but also good dispersion in water (Fig. 2F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties and peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: From the UV–vis spectra, the peak of Fe-CDs solution was focused on 337 nm. In the fluorescence spectra, Fe-CDs exhibited optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 338 nm and 436 nm, and showed a blue color under a 365 nm UV lamp ( Fig. 3 A and B). Additionally, excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of Fe-CDs could be observed in the excitation range of 360 − 480 nm (Fig. 3 C) [51]. Results in Fig. S4 and Fig. 3D demonstrated its well-resistance to various acidity conditions and ion interference. Fe-CDs remained stable even after 6 months of storage in DI water (Fig. S5). Compared to the CDs without Fe-doping, Fe-CDs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB, generating an absorption peak at 652 nm. Such phenomenon proved that Fe-CDs possessed peroxidase-like activity derived from Fe doping (Fig. 3E). By varying the mass ratios of CA and FeCl3∙6 H2O in precursors, the effects of Fe doping on peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence intensity of CDs were investigated. It was found that the peroxidase-like activity of CDs was gradually increasing with the increasing amount of Fe doping (Fig. S6). Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of CDs first increased and then decreased when the amount of FeCl3∙6 H2O kept raising (Fig. S7). We further explored the influence on fluorescence intensity of CDs between Fe-CDs and CDs physically mixed with Fe3+ under the same Fe content (Fig. S8). The results showed that the physical mixture of Fe3+and CDs resulted in fluorescence quenching because of the nonradiative electron-transfer between Fe3+ and the phenolic hydroxy groups of the CDs (Fig. S9) [46]. While, doping Fe into the CDs caused fluorescence enhancement, which could be explained by the “surface passivation-like” effects [52,53]. Further, the generation of nitrite-induced specific diazotization products was validated. Fe-CDs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB, generating a characteristic absorption peak of ox-TMB at 652 nm. When nitrite existed, nitrite could react with ox-TMB to produce diazotized ox-TMB, creating a new absorption peak at 445 nm and a reduced peak at 652 nm (Fig. 3 F) [42–45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective adsorption capacities of Fe3O4@PDA NPs on ssDNA
ce:section.ce:para: It was reported that PDA could effectively quench various fluorophores [35]. Thus, with fluorescently labeled DNA, the adsorption of DNA could be monitored by measuring the extent of fluorescence decrease [34]. Fig. 4A confirmed Fe3O4 @PDA NPs could achieve 94.5% quenching efficiency on FAM and adsorb more than 90% of FAM-ssDNA. Next, different concentrations of Fe3O4 @PDA NPs were mixed with Fe-CDs-Apt and the fluorescent signal changes in the system were recorded. With an increasing of Fe3O4 @PDA NPs concentration, the fluorescence of Fe-CDs-Apt gradually decreased, indicating that the adsorption effect of Fe3O4 @PDA NPs gradually enhanced. When Fe3O4 @PDA NPs concentration attained 250 μg/mL, its fluorescence quenching efficiency reached a plateau with 74.7%, and then kept reducing (Fig. 4B and S10). Afterwards, different adsorption capacities of Fe3O4 @PDA NPs for various conformations ssDNA were validated by Native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (Native-PAGE). By comparing the bands positions of Apt and Apt-KAN lanes, a distinct change in Apt configuration could be observed. With the addition of Fe3O4 @PDA NPs, there was no band running out on the Apt + NPs lane, while the bands of Apt-KAN + NPs did not change significantly compared to the Apt-KAN lane (Fig. 4C). Such behavior could be explained in that the bases of aptamer were buried in the backbone of Apt-KAN, attenuating the interactions with the nanocomplexes [54]. Therefore, Fe3O4 @PDA NPs did have differential affinities to ssDNA with different conformations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameters optimization for KAN and NaNO2 detection
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain favorable detection performances, several critical parameters were optimized. The F/F0 value and the A445 nm/A652 nm value were used as the main criteria for quantitative detection of KAN and NaNO2, where F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity at 436 nm of KAN-spiked and KAN-free solutions, respectively.

The secondary structure of the aptamer was susceptible to the system microenvironment, which may affect the target binding ability [55]. Hence, types of binding buffer, pH values and ion concentrations were primarily optimized. Results in Fig. S11A showed HEPES buffer could be chosen as the ideal buffer. As shown in Fig. S11B and C, when pH of the binding buffer was 7.0 and the Ca2+concentration was 2 mM, a stronger fluorescent signal-on effect could be acquired [38]. To ensure sufficient binding of aptamers to targets, it was necessary to select optimal co-incubation time of Fe-CDs-Apt with KAN. The signal dramatically increased within the first 10 min and then significantly decreased (Fig. S11D). So, 10 min was adopted for follow-up experiments. Next, a Fe3O4 @PDA NPs concentration of 250 μg/mL was employed for adequate adsorption of free Fe-CDs-Apt (Fig. S11E). And 120 s of the optimal reaction time was required for the system after the addition of Fe3O4 @PDA NPs (Fig. S11F).

Further, parameters optimization for NaNO2 detection were conducted. The detailed mechanism of nitrite-induced specific diazotization reaction was depicted in Fig. S12A. Fe-CDs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2, then nitrite could react with the aromatic primary amino group of ox-TMB to produce diazotized ox-TMB. First, 5 min of the diazotization reaction time was adopted for follow-up experiments (Fig. S12B). Also, when TMB and H2O2 substrate concentrations were 0.36 mM and 0.02 mM, respectively, the best response for NaNO2 detection was attained (Fig. S12C and D). Since antibiotics and nitrite were detected with one sensor and other aromatic carbamate antibiotics may contain in milk, interference tests were further performed, demonstrating that an accurate nitrite detection could be achieved and the nitrite-induced diazotization reaction of ox-TMB was specific (Fig. S13).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection performances of the constructed biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized conditions, the assay was applied to detect KAN and NaNO2. As presented in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant after magnetic separation kept raising as KAN concentrations increased from 17.2 pM to 858 nM. A linear plot of ΔFL (KAN-induced fluorescence intensity change at 436 nm) versus the logarithm of KAN concentration was obtained over the detection range of 17.2 pM to 343 nM, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99 (Fig. 5B). LOD (the ratio of three times standard deviation in the blank solution to the slope of the standard curve) was calculated to be 0.91 pM. The biosensor showed a relatively superior sensitivity than some other nanomaterial-based sensors for KAN detection (Table S2). Besides, when the concentration of other antibiotics including chloramphenicol (CHL), sulfadiazine (SDZ), streptomycin sulfate (SM), gentamicin (GEN) and neomycin (NEO) was 10 times higher than that of KAN, the KAN fluorescence signal response was still remarkably higher than other groups, demonstrating the good selectivity of the method for KAN analysis (Fig. 5 C).

For NaNO2 detection, Fig. 5D showed that the absorbance at 445 nm and 652 nm responded to NaNO2 concentration from 0 μM to 5000 μM. A calibration curve was calculated between the A445 nm/A652 nm value and the NaNO2 concentration over the range from 5 to 5000 μM, where the fitted linear equation could be described as y = 0.00166x + 1.1736, R2 = 0.99, with a LOD of 0.54 μM (Fig. 5E). Compared with other nanomaterial-based sensors for nitrite detection, this method still showed relatively good performance (Table S3). As well, taking some ions and amino acids in dairy products as negative controls, no significant interference was found with nitrite detection, proving good specificity of this method (Fig. 5 F and Fig. S14).

To investigate the precision of KAN and NaNO2 detection with the biosensor, the relative standard deviation (RSD) values were calculated to be 0.74% for KAN and 0.34% for NaNO2, which demonstrated the good repeatability and reproducibility of this assay (Fig. S15).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical method validation and application in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical applicability of the sensor, a classic standard addition method was employed for the quantitative determination of KAN and NaNO2 in four kinds of dairy products, including sterilized milk, pasteurized milk, fermented milk and infant milk powder [4,56]. The recoveries for spiked KAN in these samples were in the range of 92.4 − 108.7%, and the recoveries for the added NaNO2 were 90.3 − 107.2%. The RSD values were calculated to be 1.62%− 8.60% and 1.05%− 7.07%, respectively (Table S4 and S5). All these data suggested that no significant interference was encountered for KAN and NaNO2 detection in real samples, and the proposed method showed fine accuracy in practical detection.

Notably, the current national standards for food safety specify Streptococcus thermophilus inhibition method, Bacillus stearothermophilus inhibition method (GB/T 4789.27) and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (GB/T − 22969) as standard approaches for detecting KAN in dairy products, and ion chromatography and spectrophotometric methods (GB 5009.33) for nitrite detection. Comparing with the national standard methods, this assay could show relatively attractive advantages in cost, sensitivity, and detection time (Table S6).

In this study, we developed an efficient and ultrasensitive biosensor for rapid detection of antibiotics and nitrite in dairy products. The ingenious strategy and material design endowed this biosensor with a fluorescent signal and a ratiometric colorimetric signal. Fe3O4 @PDA NPs displayed high magnetic separation properties and different affinities to ssDNA with various conformations. Given that the photoluminescence property of Fe-CDs, fluorescence signal was measured to quantify KAN concentration. Combining the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs and the nitrite-induced specific diazotization reaction, NaNO2 was quantified by ratiometric colorimetry. The assay could achieve LOD values of 0.91 pM for KAN and 0.54 μM for NaNO2, and the detection of the two targets could be completed within 20 min avoiding interference from each other. Moreover, the sensor could achieve recoveries of 92.4 − 108.7% for KAN and 90.3 − 107.2% for NaNO2 in four kinds of dairy samples without obvious interference. In summary, this biosensor based on versatile CDs and Fe3O4 @PDA NPs for rapid multi-target detection holds great significance in improving the analytical efficiency of dairy monitoring. The pity is that this strategy still requires instruments to read out the signals. In our further research plan, we will try to develop portable detection devices for use with biosensors for food safety applications.

Wanchao Zuo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Wei He: Investigation. Qingqing Xiao: Investigation. Jun Yang: Resources. Xinmei Liu: Resources. Hui Jiang: Investigation. Jianjun Dai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yanmin Ju: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China [Grant number 2019YFC1605400]; the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant numbers 52002402, 31872479, 32172855], the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province [Grant number BK20200574].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132816.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

Liu, Calcium ion assisted fluorescence determination of microRNA−167 using carbon dots-labeled probe DNA and polydopamine-coated Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Mikrochim. Acta 187 (2020) 212. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00604−020−4209−8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An aqueous route to multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots using silica spheres as carriers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.L. Liu, D.Q. Wu, S.H. Liu, K. Koynov, W. Knoll, Q. Li, An Aqueous Route to Multicolor Photoluminescent Carbon Dots Using Silica Spheres as Carriers, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 48 (2009) 4598−4601. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.200900652.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence tuning in carbon dots: surface passivation or/and functionalization, heteroatom doping

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.B. Li, T. Dong, Photoluminescence tuning in carbon dots: surface passivation or/and functionalization, heteroatom doping, J. Mater. Chem. C 6 (2018) 7944−7970. https://doi.org/10.1039/c7tc05878k.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in carbon dots: from the perspective of traditional quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.H. Liu, H. Huang, W.J. Cao, B.D. Mao, Y. Liu, Z.H. Kang, Advances in carbon dots: from the perspective of traditional quantum dots, Mater. Chem. Front. 4 (2020) 1586−1613. https://doi.org/10.1039/d0qm00090f.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent oligonucleotide indicators for ratiometric microRNA sensing on metal-organic frameworks

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-H. Wang, Z.-S. Shao, C. Cheng, J.-L. Wang, Z. Song, W.-J. Song, F. Zheng, H.-S. Wang, Fluorescent oligonucleotide indicators for ratiometric microRNA sensing on metal-organic frameworks, Chem. Eng. J. 437 (2022) 135296. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.135296.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple and rapid sensing strategy based on structure-switching signaling aptamers for the sensitive detection of chloramphenicol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.Y. Ma, H.X. Li, S.N. Qiao, C.J. Huang, Q.L. Liu, X. Shen, Y.X. Geng, W.T. Xu, C.Y. Sun, A simple and rapid sensing strategy based on structure-switching signaling aptamers for the sensitive detection of chloramphenicol, Food Chem. 302 (2020) 125359. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2019.125359.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric dual signal-enhancing-based electrochemical biosensor for ultrasensitive kanamycin detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Tian, Y. Zhang, L.B. Wang, Q.J. Geng, D.X. Liu, L.L. Duan, Y.H. Wang, J.S. Cui, Ratiometric Dual Signal-Enhancing-Based Electrochemical Biosensor for Ultrasensitive Kanamycin Detection, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 12 (2020) 52713−52720. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.0c15898.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wanchao Zuo is a graduate student at China Pharmaceutical University. Her research interests focus on the design and construction of fluorescent nano-biosensors for biomedicine analysis and food quality and safety control.

Pengcheng Wu is a graduate student at China Pharmaceutical University. His research interests focus on the design and construction of lateral flow immunoassay for biomedicine analysis and food a quality and safety control.

Wei He is an associate professor of pharmacology at China Pharmaceutical University. His research interests focus on macromolecular drug delivery and cardiovascular disease therapy, biofilm-loaded drug delivery and lung disease therapy, etc.
Qingqing Xiao received her Ph.D. degree from China Pharmaceutical University in 2022, Nanjing, China. Her research interests focus on new drug dosage forms and new technologies.

Jun Yang is president of the Nanjing Institute for Food and Drug Control. His research interests focus on the monitoring of adverse reactions of drug or medical devices, and food quality and safety monitoring.
Xinmei Liu is vice president of the Nanjing Institute for Food and Drug Control. Her research interests focus on the monitoring of the quality and safety of food and cosmetics.
Hui Jiang worked at Nanjing Institute for Food and Drug Control. Her research interests focus on the design and development of biosensors for food and drug quality and safety monitoring.

Jianjun Dai is a professor of pharmacology at China Pharmaceutical University. His main research interest is pathogen infection and immunity, mainly engaged in the study of zoonotic pathogen infection and transmission mechanism.

Yanmin Ju is an associate researcher at China Pharmaceutical University. She received her Ph.D. degree from Peking University in 2019, Beijing, China. Her research interests focus on the construction and application of nanoprobes, the design and construction of nano-biosensors for biomedical analysis, and food and drug quality control.
